rxn_class_summary: Photoelectrocatalytic asymmetric heteroarylcyanation of aryl alkenes with thiophenes and TMSCN via dual acridinium photoredox / chiral copper catalysis under anodic oxidation.
electrochemical_role: Anode regenerates the ground-state acridinium photocatalyst from the acridine radical (Mes-Acr-Ph•) and re-oxidizes Cu(I) to Cu(II); cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: distal benzylic/heteroarene-stabilized radical cation (II) from alkene addition

electron_flow: Photoexcited [Mes-Acr-Ph]+* oxidizes the thiophene to a radical cation, which adds to the aryl alkene at the α-position to form a distal radical cation; deprotonation gives a benzylic radical that is captured by chiral Cu(II)(CN)2 to form a Cu(III) intermediate that undergoes enantiodetermining reductive elimination to deliver the chiral nitrile. The anode reoxidizes [Mes-Acr-Ph]• and Cu(I) back to their active states while the cathode evolves H2.
rate_determining_step: Enantiodetermining reductive elimination from Cu(III)(CN)2-alkyl complex IV (stereochemistry-determining step; not explicitly assigned as rate-limiting)

probe_reagents.name: allyl sulfone 51 (methyl 2-((phenylsulfonyl)methyl)acrylate)
probe_reagents.observation: Benzylic allylation product 52 isolated in 17% yield alongside 24% of 3, confirming a benzylic radical intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: electron-rich aryl alkene 47 (2-methoxystyrene)
probe_reagents.observation: Alkene 47 (Ep/2ox = 1.46 V) did not react with thiophene 2 (1.70 V) and decomposed; pairing 47 with more electron-rich thiophene 49 restored reactivity, supporting that the heteroarene must be more easily oxidized than the alkene.

probe_reagents.name: 2-acetylthiophene
probe_reagents.observation: Higher oxidation potential (2.19 V) than alkene 1 (2.00 V); failed to yield product 12, consistent with required photooxidation of the heteroarene.

probe_reagents.name: thiophene 2 and alkene 1 (Stern-Volmer)
probe_reagents.observation: Both quench [Mes-Acr-Ph]+*, but thiophene 2 quenches much more efficiently, indicating heteroarene is the principal SET partner.

notes: An alternative initial addition mode (II') in which the thiophene radical cation adds at the β-position of the alkene is considered but disfavored because pathway via II benefits from more extensive cation delocalization; for carbonyl-substituted alkenes (compounds 17-19), regiochemistry inverts to position (b) to avoid placing the cation adjacent to the carbonyl. Stern-Volmer data show alkene also quenches the excited state but less efficiently, so a minor alkene-first oxidation pathway cannot be strictly excluded.
